behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition
behaviors.justification: Rename detection is a git feature that requires analyzing actual git state. A pure mock that returns separate 'deleted' and 'added' entries would pass any unit test but fail to exercise the tool's rename-detection logic. The tool must actually parse git's rename detection (via --diff-filter=R or similarity threshold), which only works reliably against a real repository with actual file renames.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, commit a file, rename it, run the tool on the commit range, and verify the output contains exactly one entry in a 'renamed' category (not double-counted as deletion + addition). Cross-check with 'git diff --name-status --diff-filter=R' to confirm.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: Binary file handling is git-specific and environment-dependent. Mocking git output to include a binary file is fragile—you cannot anticipate all the edge cases (how git reports binary diffs, encoding issues, partial diff output). Must test with a real git repository containing actual binary files to catch crashes from unexpected git output or parsing failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, commit a binary file (e.g., PNG image), modify it, run the tool, and verify it completes without error and includes the binary file in the counts. Verify tool output structure is still valid JSON/structured format.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error
behaviors.justification: Requires testing with an actual git repository in the 'no commits' state. This state is difficult to mock correctly—you must test the tool's behavior when 'git log' returns nothing. A unit test with mocked git output could miss edge cases like HEAD not existing, refs not being created, or git commands failing in repo init state.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fresh git repository (git init, no commits), run the tool, and verify it produces a valid empty report (zero counts, no errors) without exiting abnormally.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed
behaviors.justification: This is a correctness oracle test that cannot be validated with mocked git output. Must create a real repository with a known sequence of operations and cross-check the tool's counts against independent git commands (e.g., 'git log --name-status' counts). Mocking git output could hide logic errors in counting, categorization, or duplicate detection.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with a known history: 5 files added, 2 deleted, 3 modified, 1 renamed across multiple commits. Run the tool, then verify reported counts by independently running 'git log --name-status' and 'git diff-tree' commands. All counts must match exactly.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: Git's staging area is complex state that cannot be reliably mocked. The tool must correctly handle the difference between 'git diff' (unstaged) and 'git diff --cached' (staged). Must test both states in a real repository to verify the tool applies the same counting/categorization logic regardless of which command is used.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, make file changes (add, delete, modify), stage some changes (git add) and leave others unstaged, run the tool twice (configured to analyze staged and unstaged respectively), and verify both produce identical output in structure and counts.
